author: @tomschwoerer
text:
I really love your designs, I'm keeping an eye open on your thingiverse channel for quite some time. I like the Ugrinsky turbine and now I'm curious, how well this one will perform, since the wind wall idea seems to be a better approach. However I'm a bit worried: 9 bearings with 8 of them rattling around freely, running on the surface of the turbine.. isn't that noisy? You can clearly hear them in the video, but it's hard to tell how loud it is. To put it into any practical use, I have to use it outside and then there are neighbors to consider. In your previous video, you showed a photo of such a  turbine which is fixed in front and back  using 2 bearings. I wonder why you went for the 9 bearings approach instead of this one? Is the efficiency loss from blocking a little wind greater than the loss due to friction of the bearings?

Once I had a similar problem to solve when I created a silent hamster wheel with 30 cm diameter, single bearing, wheel hanging freely on there and it's only held together by one screw. But even with the weight of the hamster running in the wheel the one bearing won't budge, 2 bearings should be more than enough to stabilize the turbine at high speed? Or are there other factors to consider? Vibrations?

Always happy to learn something new, thank you very much again for your great designs and videos!

author: @MarcMallary
text:
They call my bicycle hub generator a Dynamo, but It is really a Magneto, producing AC. (like your Wind Cube)
I only use it to run a headlight, which noticably flickers at very low speed,
but it doesn't seem to charge things very well.
I know there are losses from rectifying so why not use DC generators instead? 
The light is an LED so it is also a Rectifier, or possibly it's rectified before it gets to the LED.
I like your Wind Wall, because it won't kill hawks, owls, eagles,  and maybe even vultures.
I live in the California Delta, where there are large wind farms killing many birds.
I've heard that the structure of birds eyes are made for looking down and they just fly into the blades.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: well they will see this if it was a wind wall the commutation in a DC motor is often referred to as mechanical rectification

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
12v DC is a really handy output!

A whole lot od electronic machines use less than 12 volts, so one could run virtually everything in their houses with a bank of 12 volt batteries without even using AC power, save for some high drain appliances [such as washing machines, dryers, dishwashers, refrigerators, microwave ovens and the likes].

I am already gearing up to wire all of the lights on my farm to DC and have obtained 50 very good LED light strips which I can place anywhere.

And, in the mean time, since each one came with a power transformer, I can currently run it on utility power until I get all my batteries [I have 20 saved up to be restored and make a massive storage bank from] fixed and mounted, ready to be wired in.

author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
text:
Clearances, Robert, not tolerances.  Smaller clearances require higher tolerances, but it's clearances you're interested in.  Tolerances are defined in this case by the accuracy and repeatability of your 3d printer across its bed and print height and even the configuration of the object being printed.

It's a really nice looking project, and definitely worthwhile.  You might want to look at balancing the turbine once you have the magnets fixed on, your proto looks to be a bit "wobbly", and the noise it's making doesn't make me convinced it's operating mechanically as well as it potentially could.  For balancing you could do worse than using (and for once, his name not being used in crank science terms) Tesla's British patent 179,043, it's fairly simple.

author: @fredericrike5974
text: Robert, I think it was in the '90s when it was decided that the generators at Niagra Falls needed replacement or updating, they decided to update; the generators were then about a hundred years old and too big to be taken out without ripping the roof off the huge concrete structure they were in. Even with nearly ten decades of operation, they were still running very close to a mere thirty thousanths air gap all around a 25 plus foot diameter core. They put in new bearings, inspected the shafts, refreshed the windings and put them right back in to service.

author: @glencahalin4786
text:
I'm puzzled by if the parabolic front is a good or bad ideal, with an Archimedes turbine.

In a conventional bladed turbine I'd think sub-optimal,  as it's pushing the outer air more perpendicular to the tube entrance, so for conventional blades a horn shape would be better, as it'd have more air to where it works best, in that design.

 An Archimedes turbine is a different thing though, as hitting nearer the centre gives it a longer blade length to push upon.

replies.text:
Indeed it often is.
I have a whole host of "try it and see" things already half planned, for when I move out of my tiny inner city flat. Unfortunately, until then, it's much more the thinking side of things than the tinkering for me.

text: If you used floating magnetic bearings on the front and rear, your friction would drop considerably and your output would go up, also it would be silent when turning. I'm thinking of the same magnetic base of one of those so called floating pens.

replies.author: @jasonburguess
replies.text: @shotybumbati  if you use the output of the turbine as the electrical input of an electromagnet on its way to use, then the strength of the wind will be proportional to the magnetic field necessary for the bearings field. Higher velocity equals higher magnetic fields equals stronger bearings

replies.author: @shotybumbati
replies.text: @jasonburguessthen the question becomes, is the reduction of friction more beneficial than the reduced output caused by the required energy to power the bearings?

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
Great seeing all the ideas coming together from throughout the years: the wind wall, the rose, the parabolic intake, and the ring housing.

Hey, if doing a wind wall,  would it be better to have every other one spin in the opposite direction, kind of like drone propellers?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i do like it when stuff comes together - it's been a bit of a journey lol - I am not sure if opposit direction spinning bring that much to the table tbh - but worth a go as all you really have to do is change the direction of the spiral to get it to do that

replies.author: @colleenforrest7936
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I was taking a guess at stability of the wall.  Not sure how alternating vs. same direction would affect the air behind the wall or how that would affect the individual RPMs of each unit.

replies.author: @colleenforrest7936
replies.text:
I had a whole-early morning train of thought going here that YouTube just cleared out on me 😬 Doing my best to reproduce it.

If I recall correctly, in order to get a turbine to turn, you want less pressure on the back side of the turbine than is on the front, windward side.  The bigger and more  smooth that differential is, within a range that won't collapse your structure, the more RPMs you can get. But at some point the exhausts from the other fans are going to start interfering with each other.

So, if the above is true, the backend shape could be optimized to form the biggest "bubble" of smooth, low pressure air behind the blades to give better RPMs for each turbine.  Whether or not the rate of return for the effort is worth it is a different question. Also, the ideal shape may vary depending upon if you are using turbines rotating in the same or opposite directions.

Parabolas and catenaries tend to work well together in opposition, so maybe if the back end had a catenary swoop to it, that could rectify the airflow behind the wall?

This is all assuming a standard blade, though. The spiral blade may not care about what the airflow behind it looks like as much.

text:
Always very interesting, thank you.
On the subject of a 'wind wall' what do you think of hexagonal bricks? On the one had possibly a little harder to construct, but on the other the density of turbines would be higher and the structure possibly more secure.
Another potential advantage is a hexagon might lend itself more neatly to a triangular arrangement of bearings, saving one set per brick...
...or even more if the bearings are shared between bricks. Yes, that would mean having to be careful to ensure opposite turning turbines.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Hex sounds good mate - build it in hex and see

replies.author: @ijcarroll
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering  I'm in the thinking stages of building a vawt wind wall close to one of my exterior walls. The wind in my area (due to the landscape) hits this wall in 2 directions. I figure if the support for the box is made of a dowel or threaded rod, (leaving the sides open) it can catch the wind in the blowing along the wall orientation.

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering If you check the 'vidoes' tab, there are 2 each of 2312-2319.  The 1st set goes up to 2321 then it starts back over as 2312.  I believe it should have been 2322 but you may have inadvertently made a typo and then worked off of that error going forward.

author: @sarchlalaith8836
text: Would wiring the coils in 3 phase not remove the need for the capacitor?

replies.author: @Kaboom-0623
replies.text: no .... the capicitor is what is called a debounce circuit ... all phase generators have them ... because going from 0 to any voltage forces spikes .... and some of those can exceed your circuits safe limits ... for a moment ... so the capacitor catches those .... it is essentially a quick reacting short term UPS for the circuit

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Not really - you don;t have to add them if you don't want - but you will get a pretty messy output

author: @AnJo888
text: @ThinkingandTinkering do you think adding a ring shaped flywheel would help to smooth the spinning changes due to changes in the wind velocity?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: certainly - the trade off is you are going to need a greater wind velocity to overcome the flywheel inertia

replies.author: @clint9344
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Why not try implementing one of those other fancy systems you been showing to get it started..then it connects to flywheel to maintain a constant velocity..

author: @MickEMaus3000
text: Nice looking turbine!  And nice output too!  I'm curious if the turbine would stop or slow down significantly if the output wires were shorted.  There might be a need to regulate the load on the generator somehow so it doesn't draw too much from the turbine.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's not drawing directly from the turbine mate - it's drawing from the capacitor - but yes it would - they all do - it basically depnds on the size of the turbine and the load you put on it - this is a small turbine the output is never going to be that great - just because it is small

replies.author: @Madscientist233
replies.text: Yeah but the rpm required for that output is enormous. And higher resistance stators are very easily loaded down.
